text: The surgical team operates using the XI robotic system, which is visible in the operating room setup.

The surgical field displays the external iliac artery and vein, along with the obturator nerve. The lymph nodes have been removed from the surrounding area, leaving a clear view of these anatomical structures.

The surgeon displays node packets on both sides, with the bladder still elevated to show the seminal vesicles (SVs). The node packets are visible and will be sent later, remaining in the surgical field for now.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection to expose and manipulate the seminal vesicles (SVs), demonstrating a technique that may differ from standard approaches.

The surgeon identifies a consistent line approximately one centimeter above where the yellow perirectal fat meets the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder, preparing for the next step in the procedure.

The surgeon makes an initial incision about a centimeter above the yellow perirectal fat where it meets the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder. The surgeon identifies a thin, hay-like tissue layer and follows it down towards the Denonvilliers' fascia.
The surgeon navigates above the Denonvilliers' fascia, visually identifying the vas deferens first. The vas is grasped and lifted, revealing the seminal vesicles below.
The surgeon lifts the seminal vesicle (SV) and performs dissection on its downside, following the medial aspect around the corner towards the tip.
The surgeon pulls the left seminal vesicle (SV) down towards the contralateral shoulder, exposing the lateral aspect of the SV for further dissection.

The surgeon works closely along the wall of the seminal vesicle (SV), carefully hugging its surface while following the lateral aspect. The fourth robotic arm is adjusted periodically to maintain optimal positioning.
The surgeon adjusts the fourth robotic arm to improve retraction while dissecting the seminal vesicles, ensuring adequate exposure and preventing the feeling of working in a confined space.
The surgeon identifies the Denonvilliers' fascia, emphasizing the importance of staying above this structure to avoid entering the rectum during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully adjusts retraction while dissecting around the seminal vesicles (SV), ensuring precise manipulation of the tissue as the size of the SV varies.
The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope throughout the prostatectomy procedure. The bladder is visibly dropped due to the lift-nose dissection technique employed during the posterior approach.
The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia, a step that is routinely performed in this procedure, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon proceeds through the bladder neck during the prostatectomy. At the end of the procedure, during the apical dissection, the dorsal venous complex (DVC) is cut cold and then over-sewn.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the bladder neck, revealing distinct muscle fibers that are clearly visible and differentiated from the prostate tissue.

The surgeon uses the left hand to apply backward traction on the bladder neck, stretching the tissue to clearly define the muscle fibers while avoiding entry into the prostate. The right hand performs precise cutting actions.
The surgeon encounters venous bleeding near the urethra, indicating proximity to this anatomical structure during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the prostatic urethra, noting the presence of mucosal tissue with numerous veins, which causes venous bleeding, indicating proximity to the urethra.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a Foley catheter, highlighting a black line drawn at the level of the eye hole to ensure proper orientation and visibility during insertion.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a light touch with the cautery, aiming to minimize tissue charring.

The surgeon follows the bladder neck fibers, using the left hand to apply traction, which is crucial for the bladder neck step in the prostatectomy procedure. The technique involves bladder neck sparing.

The surgeon addresses the posterior bladder neck, focusing on freeing the corners to maintain tightness. A small median lobe is visible, but not prominent. Care is taken to avoid enlarging the bladder neck by carefully managing the incision edges.

The surgeon frees the corners of the posterior bladder neck, ensuring the bladder neck remains tight and does not enlarge. The corners are carefully manipulated before proceeding with cutting to maintain control over the bladder neck size.

The surgeon frees a corner of the bladder neck from the prostate and pulls it towards themselves, ensuring a tight bladder neck. The left hand is used to expose the area and apply tension, highlighting the importance of maintaining tight edges for the bladder neck.

The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction, stretching the muscle fibers to maintain the correct surgical plane. Minimal cautery is applied, causing visible contraction of the muscle fibers. The prostate tissue appears denser and marshmallow-like compared to the bladder neck fibers, which split upon cautery contact.

The surgeon navigates towards the vas deferens and seminal vesicles, ensuring to maintain a safe distance from the base of the prostate. The dissection reveals a space posteriorly, indicating prior dissection, and the presence of fat signals the transition through the bladder muscle, guiding the search for the vas deferens and seminal vesicles.
The surgeon is working within the rectal plane during the prostatectomy procedure, manipulating tissue in this anatomical area.

The surgeon navigates the perirectal fat plane, leaving the Denonvias fascia on the prostate, which provides a smoother dissection compared to removing the fascia. This approach is visually characterized by the separation of tissue layers within the perirectal fat plane.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to replicate the blunt dissection technique from open prostatectomies, entering the peri-rectal fat plane while leaving the Denonvilliers fascia on the prostate.
The surgeon makes an incision in the lateral prostatic fascia, proceeding with less aggressive nerve sparing due to the patient's lack of sexual activity.

The surgeon is working in the plane adjacent to a vein, defining the lateral edge of the prostate. The goal is to visualize the bottom edge of the prostate as it turns and to identify the perirectal fat, despite some bleeding obscuring the view.
The surgeon prepares to take the pedicle by identifying the precise location for clip placement, while managing increased urine output due to the pneumo setting.

The surgeon uses robotic clip appliers to take some of the Denonvilliers' fascia on the left side, thinning out the pedicle area by clipping vessels within the fascia. This action is performed before addressing the pedicle itself, with the fascia being more medial than the pedicle.
The surgeon places clips at the pedicle, ensuring they are positioned cranially to avoid leaving any clips near the apex where the anastomosis will be performed.

The surgeon utilizes a twelve millimeter air seal port positioned laterally to provide a clear view of the posterolateral edge of the prostate. The prostatic fascia is dissected laterally, enhancing visibility for connecting anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully approaches the pedicle near the posterolateral aspect of the prostate, ensuring nerve-sparing technique by keeping the dissection close to the prostate to avoid clipping the nerves prematurely.

The surgeon uses scissors to carefully dissect the pedicle close to the edge of the prostate, ensuring minimal tissue is left to avoid clipping the nerves. The nerves are visible as they ascend and turn to follow the posterolateral aspect of the prostate gland.

The surgeon incises the Denonvilliers fascia on the prostate to facilitate nerve sparing, leaving a square of fascia on the backside of the prostate. The anterior base tumor in the anterior transition zone allows for effective nerve sparing without significant risk.

The surgeon makes an incision on the medial side, followed by blunt dissection on the lateral aspect where the neurovascular bundle is located. The bundle is visible in the surgical field, with slight oozing due to the pneumo pressure of six.

The surgeon carefully navigates around visible veins near the prostate, avoiding aggressive dissection to prevent bleeding. The prostate's edge is identified as it begins to turn, with the perirectal fat becoming visible, indicating the location for pedicle dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the neurovascular bundle from the prostate, maintaining close proximity to the prostate tissue. The dissection plane on one side appears less defined, potentially due to previous multiple biopsies.

The surgeon navigates a stickier tissue plane on one side of the prostate, using a fenestrated Maryland bipolar instrument in the left hand to carefully dissect and manipulate the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon performs nerve sparing despite tissue stickiness, followed by cutting the dorsal venous complex (DVC) without cautery, resulting in minor bleeding. The visual focus is on the careful dissection and management of bleeding during the procedure.

The surgeon cuts closer to the prostate to avoid compromising the stitch placement, allowing for preservation of urethral length. The incision is made wider on the dorsal venous complex (DVC) to leave more tissue on the prostate, accommodating the presence of an anterior base tumor.

The surgeon cuts the dorsal venous complex (DVC) near the prostate, stopping short of the urethra to preserve its length. The incision is made to leave more tissue on the prostate side, allowing for tailored urethral length.

The surgeon cuts the posterior urethra, allowing it to retract, before addressing the posterior lip of the prostate. The urethra is now free, enabling the surgeon to inspect the area for any remaining prostate tissue.

The surgeon adjusts the incision to widen the margin without shortening the urethral length, ensuring complete resection of any posterior lip of the prostate. The prostate weighs approximately sixty grams, as indicated by the MRI. The nerve-sparing technique is employed, keeping the pedicles tight to the prostate to avoid clipping the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon places a clip distal to the node of Cloquet to prevent backflow of lymphatics from the leg. Bipolar and monopolar instruments are used for dissection, ensuring the lymphatics are securely clipped to avoid any leakage.

The surgeon clips the proximal and distal ends of the packet and uses a bipolar device in between for node dissection, avoiding monopolar due to concerns about obturator reflex and lymphoceles. Bipolar is used extensively to seal lymphatic vessels effectively.

The surgeon performs a posterior reconstruction stitch by threading through the cut edge behind the urethra, grabbing the peritoneum near the seminal vesicles, and the posterior bladder tissue. A slider knot is used to bring these tissues together, aligning the anastomosis and reducing tension.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix barbed suture for the anastomosis, noting its laser-etched barbs that do not protrude from the stitch, resembling a monocryl suture. The suture is applied to complete the anastomosis, with the surgeon preferring it for its reduced sawing effect compared to other barbed sutures.

The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundles and uses them to anchor the stitch for the anastomosis. The anastomosis is initiated at approximately the four o'clock position on the clock face, followed by two stitches on the backside to ensure a watertight closure.

The surgeon completes the posterior wall of the anastomosis first, ensuring it is watertight, before proceeding with the rest of the anastomosis. The tight space at six o'clock is addressed to prevent potential leaks.

The surgeon places a second stitch deep into the corner, securing the posterior urethra and adjacent tissue to prevent leaks. Venous oozing is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon uses the left hand to grasp the back wall of the urethra, ensuring proper anchoring without back walling.
The surgeon clips the peritoneum to close the twelve port laterally, pulling the arm back slightly to prevent potential herniation of the appendix into the port site.
